results.justification: All 5 expected rules identified (BR-1 through BR-4 as invariant: true, BR-6 weight bracket as invariant: false). State machine directionality correctly modeled — BR-2 explicitly forbids In Transit → Pending, BR-7 enumerates the only valid transitions. 14 acceptance criteria all use Given/When/Then format, far exceeding the minimum of 3. Testable assertions are concrete and developer-actionable (e.g., 'Creating or updating a shipment with weight <= 0 must be rejected'). Weight bracket correctly marked invariant: false with sound justification. Confidence level of 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions. Agent went beyond minimum requirements by adding BR-5 (weight cap as non-invariant) and BR-7 (explicit valid transition graph), plus detailed edge cases and escalations.

observations.text: Correctly split weight rules into BR-4 (weight > 0, invariant: true) and BR-5 (weight <= 1000, invariant: false with business justification) — a nuanced distinction showing domain reasoning.
BR-7 explicitly models the full directed state graph (Pending → In Transit → Delivered only), going beyond just flagging forbidden transitions to defining the complete valid transition set.
Weight bracket (BR-6) correctly classified as invariant: false with clear reasoning: 'Bracket boundaries are pricing policy. Management can adjust thresholds without corrupting system state.'
14 acceptance criteria all in proper Given/When/Then format with concrete preconditions and expected outcomes.
Edge cases are thoughtful — boundary values, floating-point precision, concurrent transitions, and idempotent same-state transitions demonstrate deep domain thinking.
Escalations correctly surface the two key ambiguities (bracket boundary ownership at 10 kg, and Pending → Delivered validity) with options and recommendations.
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with clearly separated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions.

BR-5 classifies the 1000 kg weight cap as invariant: false, which is debatable — the rubric expects weight > 0 and <= 1000 as a single invariant: true rule (BR-4 in the rubric). Splitting it and marking the cap as non-invariant is a reasonable domain interpretation but diverges from the expected answer.

results.justification: All pass criteria met: 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-7), already-shipped/in-transit edge cases (AC-2, AC-9), already-cancelled/delivered edge case (AC-4), plus authorization (AC-5), concurrency (AC-10), and failure handling (AC-8). Multiple ambiguities surfaced in both low_confidence_areas and escalations: cancellation window/eligible statuses, fee structure, who can cancel (sender vs recipient vs admin), and missing state machine definition. Confidence level is 42, well below the 65 threshold. No business rules are presented as hard facts — all are explicitly flagged as assumptions derived from domain knowledge, not from the spec. Edge cases section is thoroughly populated with 8 scenarios.

observations.text: 10 acceptance criteria far exceeds the minimum 3, covering happy path, multiple edge cases, race conditions, and failure modes
Confidence level of 42 correctly reflects the underspecified single-sentence input
4 escalations explicitly surface key ambiguities: cancellation window, fee structure, who can cancel, and missing state machine
Assumptions are clearly labeled as assumptions, not presented as facts — e.g., cancellation fees, sender authorization, post-pickup return flow
Edge cases section contains 8 well-reasoned scenarios including race conditions, network delays, batch orders, and promotional orders
Low confidence areas explicitly call out fee policy, whether in-transit orders are cancellable, recipient notification, and refund timing — all genuinely unspecified

Minor: some business rules (BR-2 sender-only, BR-7 return-to-sender flow) are marked as invariants despite being flagged as assumptions elsewhere — slight internal tension, though the assumptions section and escalations do clarify these are not confirmed

results.justification: All 4 business_impact sub-fields (financial, operational, user, risk) are substantively populated with multi-sentence analysis. stakeholders_affected lists 7 distinct groups with specific impact per group (far exceeds the 3 minimum). 3 escalations raised for missing information (delivery tier scope, business motivation, SLA contracts). Contractual SLA risk explicitly identified in both the risk field and a dedicated escalation. confidence.level of 52 is well within the <=80 threshold and appropriately calibrated given missing contract information. Competitive positioning is addressed in both financial and risk sections. Assumptions are clearly listed.

observations.text: Exceptional stakeholder breakdown with 7 distinct groups, each with specific impact descriptions — far exceeds requirements
Confidence level of 52 is well-calibrated, reflecting genuine uncertainty about contract terms and tier scope
Three distinct escalations with options and recommendations, covering tier ambiguity, missing motivation, and SLA contracts
Correctly identifies that the industry trend is toward MORE precision, framing this as a counter-trend competitive risk
Edge cases are thorough — same-day delivery incompatibility, timezone issues, API breaking changes, and premium tier degradation
Business rules include the critical insight that changing ETA granularity redefines 'on-time' measurement (BR-2)
Acceptance criteria include transition handling (AC-3) and SLA independence from display changes (AC-4)
Risk section identifies 5 distinct risks including metric gaming — artificially inflated on-time rates masking operational issues

results.justification: Bird correctly identifies the question as out-of-scope and sets confidence to 0, but still produces the full structured domain_analysis template (domain_analysis, business_rules, acceptance_criteria, edge_cases, business_impact, confidence, escalations) rather than deflecting concisely. The rubric expects Bird to respond briefly with a few sentences explaining this is outside scope and belongs to MJ or Shaq. Instead, Bird filled out every section of the domain analysis scaffold with N/A values, which is closer to 'producing a domain analysis' than 'deflecting briefly.' The escalation correctly flags out_of_scope but does not route to MJ or Shaq as expected.

observations.text: Confidence level set to 0 with clear reasoning that this is out of scope
No invented business rules, invariants, or Given/When/Then acceptance criteria
Escalation clearly identifies the question as general CS, not domain content
Still outputs the full domain analysis template structure rather than a brief deflection
Does not route to MJ or Shaq as the rubric expects — suggests 'general-purpose assistant' instead
Response is verbose (full JSON scaffold with empty/N/A fields) rather than concise (a few sentences)

results.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus a bonus BR-7). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Scheduling invariant (BR-2) marked invariant: true. Cancellation rules (BR-3, BR-4) correctly capture the role boundary between patient and admin, with BR-3/BR-4 marked invariant: false as operational policy. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-2), double-booking attempt (AC-3, AC-12), late cancellation attempt (AC-5, AC-6), and unauthorized record access (AC-8). Confidence level of 78 is within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license validation, treating relationship definition, and interval semantics.

observations.text: Invariant classifications are precise and well-justified — each invariant_justification explains why the rule is or isn't a true invariant using the 'corrupt state' test
BR-3 and BR-4 correctly split the cancellation rule into patient vs admin roles, capturing the role boundary the rubric requires
12 acceptance criteria is exceptional coverage — includes concurrent booking (AC-12), boundary conditions, and both positive and negative test cases
Edge cases are thorough and practical — boundary inclusivity, license revocation, back-to-back appointments with half-open intervals, concurrent note modification near lock deadline
Escalations identify genuine ambiguities with concrete options and recommendations, showing domain reasoning beyond surface-level rule extraction
Confidence assumptions explicitly address what 'licensed' means in the domain model, as required by the rubric
Bonus BR-7 (note editing within the 48h window) complements BR-6 by making the editable-vs-locked lifecycle explicit

results.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. Notification rule (BR-8) correctly marked invariant: false. Sanctions rule (BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Daily limit timezone ambiguity explicitly surfaced in edge_cases and escalations. 13 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). Confidence level of 68 falls within the 60-85 acceptable range with detailed assumptions listed. Bird also correctly distinguished regulatory invariants from operational/configurable rules, splitting daily limits into separate rules for verified (BR-3) and partially-verified (BR-4) tiers — both correctly marked invariant: false since the specific dollar amounts are soft config. Additionally surfaced ambiguities beyond the minimum: partially-verified withdrawal rights, notification recipient, and in-flight transaction behavior during account lock.

observations.text: All 7 required rules identified and correctly classified — no misclassifications
Notification rule (BR-8) correctly marked invariant: false with clear justification that it is a configurable business policy, not a state integrity constraint
Sanctions screening (BR-6) included as invariant: true with strong regulatory justification
Timezone ambiguity for daily limit reset explicitly called out in both edge_cases and escalations with three concrete options and a recommendation
13 acceptance criteria provided — significantly exceeds the 4 minimum, covering KYC, limits, settlement, sanctions, lockout, notifications, and counter-reset scenarios
Confidence level of 68 is well-calibrated given the ambiguities identified
Correctly distinguished partially-verified vs unverified account capabilities without conflating them
Rich edge cases including structuring detection, sanctions timing at settlement, and the exact $1,000 boundary
Escalations provide actionable options and recommendations rather than just flagging problems

BR-1 states 'full KYC verification' is required before sending, but partially-verified accounts can send up to $500/day — the rule as written contradicts BR-4. The nuance is handled in assumptions but the rule text itself is slightly imprecise

BR-3 and BR-4 are marked invariant: false for the specific dollar amounts, which is correct, but the rubric expected the existence of daily limits by tier to be marked invariant: true. Bird split the concept into amount-specific rules rather than separating the structural requirement from the threshold values

results.justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. BR-7 (refund window) correctly marked invariant: false with sound reasoning. BR-1 explicitly captures that cart-add does NOT reserve inventory. BR-2 (reservation timeout) marked invariant: false — debatable vs the rubric's 'invariant: true', but Bird's reasoning is defensible (the 15-minute duration is a tunable policy parameter, not a structural constraint). Discount/promotion mutual exclusivity captured in BR-4. Multiple ambiguities surfaced including timer semantics, delivery definition for refund window, and timezone concerns. 12 acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the minimum of 4. Confidence level of 72 is appropriate given the stated assumptions and ambiguities.

observations.text: All 7 expected business rules identified and well-articulated with strong justifications
Refund window correctly classified as invariant: false with clear policy-vs-integrity reasoning
Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1 with correct invariant: true
12 acceptance criteria — triple the minimum — covering core flows, edge cases, and concurrency
Multiple ambiguities surfaced: discount conflict resolution, price-lock semantics, timezone for refund window, delivery date definition
Rich edge cases section covering race conditions, boundary timing, and cross-cutting concerns
Comprehensive ubiquitous language glossary establishing shared vocabulary

BR-2 (15-minute reservation timeout) marked invariant: false — the rubric expects invariant: true as an inventory management rule. Bird's reasoning that the duration is tunable is valid but the rule itself (that reservations expire) is structural
Escalations section provides actionable options and recommendations for each ambiguity, not just identification

results.justification: All 7 expected rules identified (plus a bonus BR-5 for manager authorization) with correct invariant classifications. Accrual rate (false), carryover cap (false), negative balance (true), rejected-leave balance integrity (true), contract boundary (true) all correctly classified. Multiple ambiguities surfaced including contract-end-date boundary semantics and carryover interaction with pending leave — covering both expected ambiguities. 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the 4 minimum. Invariant justifications effectively distinguish structural integrity constraints from configurable HR policy. Confidence at 72 is well-calibrated given the number of genuine ambiguities identified.

observations.text: All 7 required business rules identified with correct invariant true/false classification — no misclassifications
12 acceptance criteria with clear Given/When/Then structure, covering accrual, negative balance, medical exception, carryover, and more
Rich edge cases section (10 scenarios) that surfaces real implementation risks like race conditions on concurrent approvals and year-boundary carryover

results.justification: Bird identifies the contradiction clearly and names all three sources (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3). Confidence is correctly set below 50. However, Bird produces a full domain analysis with business impact, edge cases, and — critically — a recommendation (Option C with credit notes) that constitutes an invented reconciliation. While the options are framed as possibilities rather than hard facts, the 'recommendation' line presents Option C as 'the most domain-appropriate resolution,' which crosses into inventing a reconciliation rather than purely escalating. Additionally, the acceptance_criteria array is empty (good), but the extensive analysis structure (edge cases, business impact, stakeholder analysis) goes well beyond a formal escalation — it produces a near-complete domain analysis that presumes resolution paths. The escalation is present but buried inside a full deliverable rather than being the primary response that halts work.

observations.text: All three conflicting sources (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are explicitly named and the logical contradiction is correctly articulated
Confidence level set to 40, appropriately below the 50/70 thresholds given the fundamental contradiction
acceptance_criteria array is empty — no acceptance criteria produced for the contradicted feature
Edge cases correctly marked as CONTRADICTED rather than given definitive expected behaviors
rejection_reasons section clearly states requirements cannot be implemented

Provides a concrete recommendation (Option C: credit notes) presented as 'the most domain-appropriate resolution' — this is an invented reconciliation, exactly what the rubric says should not be presented as the answer
Full domain analysis produced (ubiquitous language, business impact, stakeholder analysis) rather than a focused escalation that halts work pending stakeholder resolution
The escalation is one section among many in a comprehensive deliverable, rather than being the primary and blocking response

Reframes the underlying needs — customers want delivery confidence, not GPS; drivers want to not feel surveilled — showing genuine domain insight rather than surface-level conflict acknowledgment.
rejection_reasons array provides clear structural explanation for why the spec cannot proceed, which is an appropriate use of the output format.

results.justification: All pass criteria met: 4 bounded contexts identified (Route Planning, Fleet Management, Delivery Management, Customer Notification). Cross-context dependencies explicitly called out (e.g., vehicle class enforcement spans Fleet and Delivery contexts, route recalculation triggers depend on Delivery lifecycle events, notification triggers depend on Route state). Vehicle class constraint correctly marked invariant:true (BR-2), stop time correctly marked invariant:false (BR-7), notification window correctly marked invariant:false (BR-6). 9 acceptance criteria provided (well above the 4 minimum). All 3 route recalculation triggers enumerated (new delivery, failed delivery, manual deviation) in BR-3/4/5.

observations.text: Exceptional bounded context identification — 4 contexts with clear ownership boundaries and explicit integration seam commentary
10 business rules identified, far exceeding the expected 4, with well-reasoned invariant classifications and justifications
9 detailed Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy paths, vehicle class filtering, all recalculation triggers, notifications, and stop time overrides
10 edge cases surfaced including concurrent recalculation, notification deduplication, GPS drift, and shift boundaries — strong defensive analysis
Escalations are well-structured with options and recommendations, correctly identifying multi-driver partitioning and deviation detection as critical ambiguities
Discovered additional invariants not in the rubric (single active route per driver BR-9, single route per delivery BR-10) that are domain-correct

Route recalculation triggers (BR-3/4/5) marked as invariant:false — the rubric expected invariant:true since the system must always recalculate on these events. Bird's justification (they're optimization policies, not data integrity constraints) is defensible but diverges from the expected answer

results.justification: All pass criteria met: Breaking change clearly identified (1:1 to 1:N cardinality change called out in business_context and throughout). New minimum-cardinality invariant stated (BR-1: at least one recipient). Data migration impact for 2M records noted (BR-5, AC-7, business_impact.risk). External API breakage identified (BR-7, AC-4, escalations, stakeholders). 'Delivery preferences' ambiguity surfaced (escalation #3 explicitly asks what preferences mean). Architectural concerns flagged via escalations (fulfillment model, pricing, state machine). Confidence level 52, well under 75 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough breaking change analysis — identifies not just the cardinality change but cascading impacts on billing, routing, notifications, and reporting
BR-1 clearly states the minimum-cardinality invariant with strong justification
BR-5 explicitly addresses lossless migration of 2M records with a testable assertion comparing pre/post-migration data
BR-7 and AC-4 address external API backward compatibility with concrete strategy (Primary Recipient in legacy responses)
Escalation #3 directly surfaces the delivery preferences ambiguity with three concrete options

Stakeholder analysis is comprehensive — identifies 8 affected groups with specific impacts for each

results.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 as invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 as invariant:false). Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both edge_cases and escalations (mid-dunning tier change interaction). Recommendation to store policies as configuration is embedded in testable assertions using named constants (RENEWAL_LEAD_DAYS, MAX_RENEWAL_RETRIES, PREMIUM_GRACE_DAYS).

observations.text: All 6 classifications correct with no errors

results.justification: All six pass criteria met: (1) Context coupling explicitly identified as a domain model change — BR-6, the escalation on contradiction, and the risk assessment all frame this as coupling two previously independent bounded contexts. (2) Data ownership question raised — BR-1 explicitly states StockLevel is owned by Context A, and BR-6 requires an Anti-Corruption Layer so Context B doesn't adopt Warehouse terminology. (3) TOCTOU/simultaneous-order race condition identified — BR-5 directly names the check-then-act problem across contexts, AC-3 tests the concurrent order scenario, and the edge case 'Stock drops to 0 between advisory check and warehouse pick' is the textbook TOCTOU pattern. (4) Stock reservation vs. check distinction surfaced — the Reservation term is explicitly called out as MISSING from both contexts, BR-5 distinguishes advisory checks from authoritative fulfillment, and the escalation recommends advisory check now with reservation pattern as follow-up. (5) Escalation to MJ for architectural coupling pattern — the first escalation directly addresses the architectural question with three options (advisory, synchronous, reservation saga). (6) New cross-context invariant stated — BR-5 is the new cross-context invariant, and AC-3 encodes it as a testable scenario.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough TOCTOU analysis — doesn't just mention the race condition but explains why the advisory check is fundamentally non-authoritative (BR-5) and provides a concrete concurrent-order acceptance criterion (AC-3)
Reservation concept explicitly flagged as missing from both contexts, with clear escalation recommending advisory check now and reservation saga as follow-up — exactly the right sequencing
Anti-Corruption Layer requirement (BR-6) goes beyond the rubric expectations by identifying model-level coupling risk, not just runtime coupling
Escalation options are well-structured with concrete tradeoffs — Option A (advisory), B (synchronous), C (reservation saga) — and a clear recommendation to reject Option B
Edge cases identify several undefined policies (partial vs atomic order blocking, blocked order expiration) and correctly escalate rather than silently resolving them
AC-6 addresses graceful degradation during Warehouse outage — a failure mode not in the rubric but critical for a cross-context integration
Low confidence score (45) is appropriate given the number of unresolved ambiguities and the fundamental contradiction in the feature request
Rejection reasons act as guardrails preventing the exact failure modes the rubric warns about — direct DB access, assuming authoritative checks, shared models without ACL

results.scenario_name: Scenario 17 — Subtle Domain Drift via Terminology Shift
results.justification: Bird explicitly identifies the terminology collision between Quote 'confirmed' and Order 'confirmed', recommends distinct names ('accepted', 'verbally_agreed', 'customer_approved', 'terms_accepted'), identifies the payment-bypass risk in detail (edge cases about fulfillment triggering on unpaid quotes), and clearly explains the domain drift. However, the output contains zero Given/When/Then acceptance criteria — the acceptance_criteria array is empty. The rubric requires at least 2 Given/When/Then criteria for the quote-to-order transition, which is a hard requirement for pass.

observations.text: Terminology collision identified explicitly and analyzed in depth — escalation section clearly states the two definitions cannot coexist under the same term

Domain drift explained clearly — the analysis distinguishes between the valid intent (tracking verbal agreements) and the problematic terminology choice
Excellent stakeholder impact analysis covering Sales, Finance, Warehouse, Engineering, and Customers
Zero Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided — the acceptance_criteria array is completely empty, failing a mandatory rubric requirement

results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Jurisdiction-specific rules identified for all 3 jurisdictions — CA §226 wage statement requirements, DE works council co-determination gate, ID contractor classification with distinct fee terminology. (2) Germany works council approval explicitly identified as a hard deployment gate/pre-condition (BR-4, AC-2). (3) Driver classification data model difference clearly identified — employee (CA/DE) vs contractor (ID) with distinct labeling requirements (BR-5, AC-4). (4) Formal escalation for legal review present across 4 detailed escalations covering deduction terminology, real-time definition, AB5/Prop 22 status, and Betriebsrat timeline. (5) No single global acceptance criteria set — ACs are jurisdiction-specific throughout. (6) Confidence level is 52, well below the 60 threshold.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on all three jurisdictions — California §226 statutory penalties quantified, Germany BetrVG §87(1)(6) cited precisely, Indonesia misclassification risk identified as a data model and labeling concern
Correctly identified the subtle trap: contractor terminology mimicking employee wage statements creates misclassification litigation risk (BR-5)
Works council approval modeled as a hard deployment gate with a feature flag assertion (AC-2), not just a soft mention
Driver relocation edge case (ID→CA) captures the reclassification data model challenge — historical records retain contractor terminology while new records switch to employee terminology
AB5/Prop 22 escalation shows deep legal awareness — correctly notes the constitutional challenge is under appeal and recommends the conservative path
Works council conditions modeled as potentially reshaping the feature (BR-8, AC-3), not just a binary approve/deny gate — shows understanding of co-determination scope
Phased rollout recommendation embedded in escalation 4 — build CA and ID first, DE only after Betriebsrat conditions are known
Confidence reasoning is well-justified, citing specific gaps (exact deduction categories, Betriebsrat conditions, Prop 22 status) rather than generic uncertainty

results.justification: All pass criteria met: PM/Legal contradiction explicitly identified and escalated as the first and most critical escalation; clock-start ambiguity identified in assumptions and edge cases; wrong-address exception from Finance noted as BR-6 with edge case coverage; auto-refund vs 3-day processing conflict identified as a formal escalation; 8 rules extracted with correct invariant classifications; 5 formal escalations provided; confidence.level is 35, well below the 50 threshold.

observations.text: PM/Legal contradiction is not just identified but elevated as CRITICAL with three resolution options and a nuanced hybrid recommendation — exactly the kind of domain authority Bird should demonstrate.
Clock-start ambiguity captured in assumptions ('inferred from Operations Lead's comment but not explicitly stated') and implicitly in the pickup SLA escalation.
Wrong-address exception surfaced as BR-6 and further explored in edge cases and a dedicated escalation about the adjudication process — goes beyond just noting it.
Auto-refund vs 3-business-day processing conflict is a standalone escalation with clear options, satisfying the rubric requirement.
8 business rules extracted, all with correct invariant classifications. BR-1 through BR-3 correctly marked as invariants; BR-4 through BR-8 correctly marked as non-invariants.
5 formal escalations covering contradiction, ambiguity, and missing context — well above the 2 minimum required.
Confidence at 35 with strong reasoning about why it's low. High/low confidence areas are well-differentiated.
Rejection reasons clearly state why implementation cannot proceed, reinforcing that Bird refuses to produce a 'clean' model when the domain is contradictory.
Ubiquitous language includes 'Goodwill Refund' explicitly marked as DISPUTED TERM — demonstrates Bird's refusal to silently resolve contradictions.
Edge cases are thorough and practical, covering boundary conditions (exactly 10km), mid-delivery rating drops, and multi-cancellation scenarios.
